html:
AQA GCSE English Literature — Worlds and Lives Anthology — 10 Most Likely Comparison Questions to Come Up (2026)

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section B (Anthology Poetry) Q24. 30 marks. Recommended writing time: 45 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context). AQA prints ONE named anchor poem from the 15 in full on the paper; the student selects ONE OTHER poem from the anthology to compare. Stem reads: "Compare how poets present [THEME] in '[NAMED ANCHOR POEM]' and in one other poem from 'Worlds and lives'." — AQA supplies the named poem; the student picks the comparison.

How this list was generated

This Q-bank was built using Sophicly's AQA prediction algorithm adapted for the Worlds and Lives cluster's new-spec status (see `Worlds-and-Lives-AQA-Prediction-Algorithm.md` in this folder). The cluster was added to the AQA spec in 2023 and first examined in summer 2024, leaving only two live papers (2024 + 2025) — both with LOW triangulation confidence because AQA filestore returns image-only PDFs that no extractable-text method has yet recovered. The algorithm therefore swaps the per-text history-driven signals for the new-spec substitute-scoring approach: SpecAOWeight (0.30), SpecimenPaperMatch (0.35), AnalogousTextPattern against Power and Conflict (0.15), SiblingBoardSignal (0.10), and CoreTopicBoost (0.10) for the cluster's five core poems — Lines Written in Early Spring (Wordsworth — Romantic-roots), In a London Drawingroom (Eliot — Victorian-roots), A Portable Paradise (Robinson — diaspora-archive), Thirteen (Femi — race / state-contact), and With Birds You're Never Lonely (Antrobus — contemporary climate / non-human-consciousness). RecencyPenalty is light in a two-paper window but still active.

Section IDs (Q1–Q10) ARE the priority order. Q1 = highest-confidence anchor, Q10 = lowest within the top 10. Confidence labels in section headers are derived from the algorithm's substitute-scoring output, not from observed return cycles (which the cluster's thin history does not yet support).

Priority order — predicted 2026 named-anchor rankings

This table ranks our predictions for which Worlds and Lives poem AQA will print as the named anchor in 2026. The Q-bank below rehearses Sophicly's suggested comparison for each predicted anchor, but the student is always free to substitute a different second poem from the anthology.

| Algorithm rank | Predicted named anchor (AQA prints in full) | Confidence | Likely 2026 controlling theme | Sophicly's suggested comparison poem |

|---:|---|---|---|---|

| 1 | A Portable Paradise (Robinson, 2019) | HIGH | Belonging / inherited home | Homing (Berry) |

| 2 | Thirteen (Femi, 2020) | HIGH | Race / state contact / urban Black-British identity | On an Afternoon Train (Berry) |

| 3 | Lines Written in Early Spring (Wordsworth, 1798) | HIGH | Place / nature as moral teacher | In a London Drawingroom (Eliot) |

| 4 | In a London Drawingroom (Eliot, 1869) | HIGH | Urban experience / refusal of nature | Lines Written in Early Spring (Wordsworth) |

| 5 | With Birds You're Never Lonely (Antrobus, 2019) | MED-HIGH | Loneliness / non-human consciousness | Lines Written in Early Spring (Wordsworth) |

| 6 | Homing (Berry, 2014) | MED-HIGH | Language / inheritance / voice | A Portable Paradise (Robinson) |

| 7 | Like an Heiress (Nichols, 2020) | MED | Climate / Caribbean inheritance | A Portable Paradise (Robinson) |

| 8 | England in 1819 (Shelley, 1819) | MED | Power / state failure | Thirteen (Femi) |

| 9 | Name Journeys (Mundair, 2003) | MED | Identity / language reclamation | Homing (Berry) |

| 10 | On an Afternoon Train (Berry, 1990) | MED | Migration / cross-racial recognition | A Portable Paradise (Robinson) |

Coverage Pack (rehearse but not top-10):

• A century later (Dharker) — strong on conflict / generation themes; Malala intertext gives standalone weight.

• A Wider View (Seneviratne) — strong on family / labour history; Marshall's Temple Mill specificity.

• The Jewellery Maker (Parker) — strong on craft / rural Black presence.

• pot (Khan) — strong on object-poetics / postcolonial museum critique.

• Shall earth no more inspire thee (Brontë) — strong on place-as-speaker apostrophe.

How to use this document

How the AQA stem works. AQA Paper 2 Section B Q24 names ONE poem from the 15-poem anthology in the question stem and prints that poem in full on the exam paper. The student then selects ONE OTHER poem from the anthology — from memory — to compare. The stem is fixed in shape: "Compare how poets present [THEME] in '[NAMED ANCHOR POEM]' and in one other poem from 'Worlds and lives'. [30 marks]". AQA supplies the named poem and the controlling theme; the student picks the comparison.

What this Q-bank predicts. Each of the 10 questions below predicts the LIKELY named anchor for 2026 and pairs it with Sophicly's recommended comparison poem — the strongest defensible pairing under the predicted theme. The recommended comparison is NOT what AQA pairs (AQA pairs nothing; the student chooses) — it is the comparison we judge most rewarding for that anchor under that theme.

Each Q prints:

• The AQA-format stem with the predicted named anchor and predicted controlling theme.

• A Suggested comparison poem line — Sophicly's primary recommendation with a one-line rationale.

• An Alternative comparison candidates line — two or three other defensible pairings the student may prefer.

• A frame paragraph, a technique gloss, and a skeleton plan in Sophicly's locked comparison structure (Introduction 3 sentences / three Body Paragraphs of 7 sentences / Conclusion 4 sentences) adapted for poetry comparison.

Plan structure. Each plan follows AQA's correct integrated-comparison shape for anthology poetry: BP1 = FORM comparison (both poems woven together), BP2 = STRUCTURE comparison (both woven), BP3 = LANGUAGE comparison (both woven). Every BP uses Comparative TTECEA+C — every sentence compares both poems. If the student substitutes a different comparison from the alternative-candidates list, the form / structure / language axis stays; only the specific techniques and evidence change.

Rehearsal priority. If exam time is tight, rehearse the top 5 first (Q1–Q5). For comprehensive coverage, rehearse all 10. Memorise the cross-question reuse table at the end so the same prepared plans answer multiple Qs.

Pedagogical depth — three non-negotiables for Worlds and Lives

Every Grade-9 essay on a W&L comparison should thread at least one of these moves into one BP or the conclusion. They are the anthology's deepest analytical pivots; Sophia teaches them once so students can deploy them across any Q.

1. Identity is NOT a destination — it is an INTERNAL ARCHIVE carried. The anthology's deepest editorial move.

The standard reading of Worlds and Lives treats the anthology as a comparison-set on identity, place, heritage, and diaspora. The sharper second reading exposes the anthology's deepest editorial argument: identity is not a destination but an INTERNAL ARCHIVE CARRIED. The teaching move is to replace place-as-identity with archive-as-identity. Robinson's A Portable Paradise (2019) names this most clearly: paradise is "a small folded sheet of paper / and a clear plastic bag" carried "in the secret compartment of my inside-jacket pocket"; the closing command "polish it… and let it shine like a brilliant amulet" names the inheritance as a portable practice, not a recoverable location. Femi's Thirteen (2020) locates the Black-British boy's interior — "you become a master of disguise" — as the archive on which survival depends; identity is the speaker's interior management of a hostile public, not a Peckham address. Berry's Homing (2014) carries the Black Country dialect "in a box beneath the bed" until the homing-pigeon image returns the inheritance through the body. Mundair's Name Journeys (2003), Khan's pot (postcolonial museum critique), and Antrobus's With Birds You're Never Lonely (deaf attention) all locate heritage as something INSIDE the speaker, not outside. The anthology's editorial work is to replace the fixed citizenship-document picture of belonging with the active practice-and-inheritance picture: paradise is not WHERE you are; it is WHAT YOU CARRY. Thread this into: Q1 A Portable Paradise / Q2 Thirteen / Q6 Homing / Q7 Like an Heiress / Q9 Name Journeys / Q10 On an Afternoon Train / any conclusion that names the anthology's contribution to contemporary British identity-debate.

2. The Romantic-roots poems anticipate the contemporary speakers — British poetry has been processing displacement long before twentieth-century migration.

The standard reading splits the anthology into a Romantic-roots half (Wordsworth, Brontë, Shelley, Eliot) and a contemporary half (Robinson, Femi, Berry, Mundair, Antrobus, Khan, Nichols, Seneviratne, Parker, Dharker). The sharper second reading exposes the editorial argument: the Romantic-roots poems present THE DISPOSSESSED OBSERVER as the precursor to the contemporary diaspora speaker. Wordsworth's 1798 grove in Lines Written in Early Spring — "To her fair works did Nature link / The human soul that through me ran" — places the speaker outside the political nation, working through displacement-from-the-inherited-order by means of nature. Eliot's In a London Drawingroom (1869) locates the Victorian woman as the dispossessed observer behind glass — "the world seems one huge prison-house" — whose vantage point anticipates the displacement contemporary speakers will name more openly. Shelley's England in 1819 indicts the Regency state as a system that has exiled its own people through "old, mad, blind, despised, and dying" rule. Brontë's Shall earth no more inspire thee (apostrophe — direct address to an absent listener — to the moor) locates the speaker outside the home she addresses. The Romantic-roots half therefore does not merely contrast with the contemporary half; it ANTICIPATES the contemporary speakers' structural position. British poetry has been processing displacement since the late eighteenth century — the diaspora speaker's experience extends a tradition the dominant culture had previously read as Romantic interiority. The anthology's historical span argues that displacement is not unique to twentieth-century migration but is the through-line of British lyric across its modern period. Thread this into: Q3 Lines Written in Early Spring / Q4 In a London Drawingroom / Q8 England in 1819 / any BP3 that pairs a Romantic-roots poem with a contemporary poem (e.g. Wordsworth with Antrobus, Eliot with Femi).

3. Name WHICH world, name WHICH life — the analytical precondition.

The anthology's title is plural on both sides — Worlds and Lives — and the editorial argument is that there is no single British world and no single British life. The anthology's worlds include the Romantic nation (Wordsworth's 1798 grove, Shelley's 1819 Regency state), the Victorian city (Eliot's London drawing-room), the post-Windrush commuter route (Berry's 1955 train), the diaspora interior (Robinson's pocket-paradise), the Black-British urban estate (Femi's Peckham), the colonised place re-claimed (Khan's pot), the climate-vulnerable coast (Nichols's Atlantic Guyana), the deaf-attentive sanctuary (Antrobus's Zealandia). The lives the anthology examines are the identities formed across these worlds: the Caribbean grandmother's heir, the bilingual British-Punjabi child, the Black-British thirteen-year-old, the Black-British rural Jewellery maker, the deaf Jamaican-British poet. Every comparison essay should name in its thesis WHICH world and WHICH life each poem inhabits and HOW the two worlds and the two lives produce different angles on the main idea. Generic "identity and belonging" answers cap at Band 4. Top-band answers specify and distinguish. Hall's "positioning" frame (1990) and Gilroy's Black Atlantic frame (1993) travel cleanly across the anthology's contemporary half; Robinson's "internal archive" is the anthology's own version of the same move.

Why this depth matters pedagogically. AQA's Band 6 descriptor asks for "a convincing, critical analysis and exploration" of method, sustained across the comparison. A reading that names archive-as-identity (not place-as-identity), names the Romantic-roots half as the contemporary speakers' precursor, and specifies WHICH world and WHICH life is the kind of thinking Examiner Reports name as the top-band signature. Without it, a comparison essay is parallel description; with it, the essay is critical analysis.

A universal method for finding the second reading. Every text in the AQA specification rewards the same move: identify the standard reading; then identify a second reading the same evidence supports; then read the two together. For Worlds and Lives the paired readings are place-as-identity/archive-as-identity, Romantic-roots-vs-contemporary/Romantic-roots-as-contemporary-precursor, and generic-identity/specified-world-and-life. Sophia should generate the second reading whenever helping a student plan — the standard reading is what most students will write; the second reading is what carries them past the band-9 line.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape — AQA integrated-comparison shape for W&L

BP1 = FORM comparison (both poems woven together). BP2 = STRUCTURE comparison (both woven). BP3 = LANGUAGE comparison (both woven). Every BP uses Comparative TTECEA+C — every element compares both poems within the same move. Within each BP:

1. Comparative topic sentence — concept-led; explicit "While X..., Y..." contrast naming the form / structure / language axis. No techniques named yet.

2. Comparative technique — the form-related, structure-related, or language-related techniques in BOTH poems, named with sophisticated literary terms (free indirect discourse, anaphora, apostrophe, dramatic monologue, blank verse, code-switching, second-person address, in medias res, praeteritio, pathetic fallacy).

3. Comparative evidence — anchor quotes from BOTH poems held in the same move.

4. Comparative close analysis — word-level analysis on specific verbs / nouns / images from both anchor quotes, weaving the two.

5. Comparative effects — what the reader experiences in each poem, compared. Use specific emotion words (recognition, complicity, suffocation, satisfaction, indictment, tenderness, exposure, integration). Avoid generic "sadness" / "interest".

6. Comparative author's purpose — why each poet made that form / structure / language choice, compared. Hedged ("may have chosen", "can be read as choosing", "a defensible reading is", "perhaps").

7. Comparative context (AO3) — period-specific historical / cultural / institutional facts for both poets, compared. Each context drives that poet's specific choice (context shapes concept which produces method).

Memorise these once

The Worlds and Lives anthology argues that identity and belonging are worked out in the spaces between worlds — geographic, generational, linguistic, historical, and ecological — and that the question of who counts as British, who can speak in English, and whose worlds matter is reopened in each of fifteen poems from a different angle. Across poems written between 1798 and 2020, the anthology traces this argument from the Romantic criticism of the British political and natural order (Wordsworth, Shelley, Brontë) through the Victorian study of urban refusal (Eliot) into post-Windrush diasporic recognition (Berry's 1955 train) and finally into the contemporary reopening of belonging through dialect (Berry's Homing), bilingual inheritance (Mundair), object-poetics of colonial extraction (Khan), the family archive (Seneviratne), the schoolroom under threat (Dharker), rural craft (Parker), deaf attention (Antrobus), the grandmother's instruction (Robinson), the climate-vulnerable coast (Nichols), and the racialised state encounter (Femi). Two main tensions run through the anthology: SCALE (whose world the speaker inhabits and which life that world produces) and TIME (whether the inherited place must be returned to or whether it travels with the speaker as portable internal archive). Every Grade-9 comparison should hold both.

Key terms (memorise once — useful across multiple Qs):

• Apostrophe — direct address to an absent or non-human addressee (Brontë's Shall earth no more inspire thee gives the moor a speaking voice; Khan's pot gives the museum object a voice).

• Code-switching — alternation between two languages or registers inside a single text (Berry's Black Country dialect inside standard-English syntax; Mundair's Punjabi-into-English).

• Blank verse — unrhymed iambic pentameter; the form Milton and Wordsworth used for epic and philosophical content (Eliot deploys it for a confined drawing-room — the mismatch is the argument).

• Polysyndetic / asyndetic catalogue — accumulation of items with / without conjunctions (Shelley's "old, mad, blind, despised, and dying" piles negative adjectives without coordinated relief).

• Pathetic fallacy — natural phenomena attributed human emotion (Wordsworth's flowers that "enjoy the air"; Brontë's moor that addresses the wanderer).

• In medias res — opening mid-action without scene-setting (Femi's "He was thirteen / walking home from school").

• Second-person address — speaker addresses the reader as "you" (Femi's "you become a master of disguise"; Berry's grandmother spoken to as "you").

• Free indirect discourse — third-person narration that holds the character's voice without quotation marks (Seneviratne's "craving the comfort of a wider view").

• Praeteritio — Latin for saying something by saying you will not say it (Shelley's "I shall not catalogue all our failures" job performed by the sonnet's catalogue itself).

• Dramatic monologue — verse spoken by a constructed persona whose worldview the form exposes (Brontë's moor as speaker; Khan's pot as speaker).

Cluster-specific framing (memorise once — threads into every W&L BP):

• The anthology's editorial argument — the anthology was built to widen which voices count as British literary speech. Placing James Berry's Caribbean voice next to Wordsworth's Romantic blank verse, Caleb Femi's Peckham boyhood next to Emily Brontë's Yorkshire moors, or Shamshad Khan's Manchester-Asian object lyric next to Roger Robinson's Trinidadian portable-paradise sermon, is itself a contextual factor the essay can write about.

• Hall and Gilroy on diaspora positioning — identity is "always a positioning" (Hall 1990); Black-British culture is fundamentally "Atlantic and transnational" (Gilroy 1993). Use sparingly in BP3 or conclusion of any contemporary-half comparison.

• Robinson's "internal archive" — heritage is an internal archive carried, not a destination. Read contemporary poems' identity politics through this lens.

• The Romantic criticism of authority — Wordsworth and Shelley wrote during the half-century after the French Revolution in which inherited authority was being stripped of legitimacy. Their poems share a refusal to accept that any inherited structure (monarchy, urban order, parliamentary settlement) is permanent or innocent.

• Post-Windrush British identity reopening — the 2017–18 Windrush scandal, the 2020 Black Lives Matter UK mobilisation, the post-2016 EU referendum debate on British identity, and the contemporary museum-decolonisation debate all sit inside the anthology's editorial moment.

---

Q1 — A Portable Paradise (predicted named anchor) [HIGH confidence — core poem · diaspora-archive + grandmother-inheritance]

Extract

Anchor poem (printed on the AQA paper):

And if I speak of Paradise,

then I'm speaking of my grandmother

who told me to carry it always

on my person, concealed, so

no one else would know but me.

That way they can't steal it, she'd say.

And if life puts you under pressure,

trace its ridges in your pocket,

smell its piney scent on your handkerchief,

hum its anthem under your breath.

And if your stresses are sustained and daily,

get yourself to an empty room – be it hotel,

hostel or hovel – find a lamp

and empty your paradise onto a desk:

your white sands, green hills and fresh fish.

Shine the lamp on it like the fresh hope

of morning, and keep staring at it till you sleep.

— Roger Robinson, A Portable Paradise (2019)

Suggested comparison poem (student choice — pick any other Worlds and Lives poem): Homing by Liz Berry. Why: matches the grandmother-as-inheritance-source angle this Q rewards, and pairs the diaspora-archive (Robinson) with the dialect-archive (Berry) — both inheritances matrilineal, both portable, both refusing the picture of belonging as passport or birth certificate.

Alternative comparison candidates:

• Name Journeys (Mundair) — bilingual interior carried as identity-archive; same internal-archive logic via Punjabi/English doubling.

• Like an Heiress (Nichols) — Caribbean coastal inheritance walked rather than carried; same Atlantic-archive frame under different contemporary pressure.

• On an Afternoon Train (Berry) — dignity in the diaspora as small public choice; Caribbean inheritance held against the British public sphere.

Question

Compare how poets present ideas about belonging in 'A Portable Paradise' and in one other poem from 'Worlds and lives'. [30 marks]

Frame

Robinson's 2019 single-stanza meditation and Berry's 2014 dialect-recovery lyric both present belonging as inheritance from a grandmother figure carried into the speaker's present, but their inheritances differ in tone: Robinson's grandmother gives a meditative practice ("polish it… and let it shine like a brilliant amulet"), while Berry's grandmother gives a stigmatised dialect ("kept your accent / in a box beneath the bed"). The plan traces three layers of the comparison — form (single-stanza instruction versus bilingual free verse), structure (unbroken transmission versus loss-and-return), and language (the amulet image versus the homing-pigeon image).

Pedagogical depth to thread. Name WHICH world, name WHICH life. Robinson's world is the Trinidadian-British diaspora interior carried in an inside jacket pocket; the life is the diasporic adult honouring grandmother-wisdom across the Middle Passage. Berry's world is the Black Country dialect community before and after the schoolroom; the life is the working-class woman re-claiming her voice after institutional suppression. Both inheritances are passed down through the mother's line and portable rather than national and fixed — and the anthology's editorial argument that belonging is plural rather than centred runs through both poems. Land this in BP3 or the conclusion. Hall's "positioning" frame works cleanly here.

Technique gloss:

• Single-stanza free verse (Robinson) — instruction delivered without sectional breaks; the form refuses interruption.

• Imperative mood (Robinson) — "take", "keep", "polish", "let it shine" — direct instruction to the speaker and through him to the reader.

• Simile (Robinson) — "like a brilliant amulet" — small protective inherited object.

• Bilingual free verse (Berry) — Black Country dialect words ("bostin'", "cag-mag") integrated unitalicised inside standard-English syntax.

• Clothing metaphor (Berry) — "how I wear my voice" — voice as garment.

• Homing-pigeon metaphor (Berry) — "my tongue / a flock of homing pigeons" — the dialect returning the way pigeons home to a loft.

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): On 22 June 1948, the MV Empire Windrush docked at Tilbury. In the 2017-18 Windrush scandal, Caribbean-British citizens who had arrived as children were wrongly classified as illegal immigrants and in some cases deported. The public question of who counts as belonging in Britain has been continually reopened. Robinson's 2019 poem, written shortly after he emerged as a major voice of the Grenfell Tower fire of 2017, answers the reopening by carrying his Trinidadian grandmother's instruction into the British present.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Berry, writing five years earlier in 2014 from the post-1944-Education-Act Black Country, used the homing-pigeon image to name a different but nearby inheritance. Received Pronunciation had been systematically promoted in state schools as the prestige variety, and regional dialects had been trained out of working-class children. The dialect was carried in the body across the schoolroom that had tried to remove it. Both poets locate belonging not in citizenship documents but in the inheritance a grandmother carried forward — refusing the picture of belonging as either passport or birth certificate.

• Thesis: Across form (single-stanza unbroken instruction versus bilingual free verse), structure (continuous transmission versus loss-and-return), and language (the amulet image versus the homing-pigeon image), Robinson presents belonging as a meditative practice inherited from a grandmother and carried internally across the diaspora, while Berry presents belonging as a stigmatised dialect reclaimed from a grandmother whose accent the schoolroom had locked in a box. Both poets together argue that the place a person belongs to is something a previous generation handed forward rather than something the citizenship register confirms.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Robinson chooses a single-stanza meditative free-verse form to present belonging as an unbroken grandmotherly instruction the diaspora preserved, Berry chooses a stanza-broken bilingual free-verse form to present belonging as a dialect the institution broke and the speaker must recover.

• Comparative technique: Robinson's form holds the shape of the grandmotherly counsel-poem inside one continuous stanza of imperative meditation, whereas Berry's form holds the shape of the bilingual elegy inside stanzas that absorb Black Country dialect words unitalicised inside standard-English syntax.

• Comparative evidence: Robinson opens "And if I speak of Paradise, / then I'm speaking of my grandmother" and closes "polish it / and let it shine like a brilliant amulet" inside one unbroken stanza, while Berry opens "For years you kept your accent / in a box beneath the bed" and breaks into the closing pigeon image "my tongue / a flock of homing pigeons" across stanzas that name suppression and return.

• Comparative close analysis: Robinson's choice of the counsel-poem form makes the absence of stanza breaks itself an argument — the grandmother's voice is never interrupted because the diaspora preserved it — while Berry's choice of the bilingual-elegy form makes the stanza breaks themselves an argument, each break marking a phase (suppression, leakage, recovery) the dialect had to survive.

• Comparative effects: Thus the reader of Robinson receives belonging as continuous responsibility passed across one breath, while the reader of Berry experiences belonging as the satisfaction of an inherited voice recovered after damage — discipline-of-practice contrasted with relief-of-homecoming.

• Comparative author's purpose: Robinson may have chosen the meditative single-stanza form to argue that diasporic inheritance is a practice the speaker cannot afford to interrupt, while Berry can be read as choosing the bilingual-elegy form to argue that the institutional suppression of dialect must be named through the stanza-architecture itself before the recovery can land.

• Comparative context: Robinson writes inside the long Caribbean tradition of grandmother-wisdom poetry — Grace Nichols's I Coming Back, James Berry's Lucy's Letters, Olive Senior's Talking of Trees — that treats the elderly woman as the main carrier of cultural inheritance across the Middle Passage, while Berry writes inside the post-1944-Education-Act recovery of regional voice that the tripartite-secondary-system schoolroom had drilled out of Black Country children for half a century.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Robinson structures the poem as one continuous imperative transmission moving from grandmother to speaker to reader, Berry structures the poem as a phased loss-and-return moving from suppression through leakage to recovery.

• Comparative technique: Robinson deploys cumulative imperative parallelism ("trace its ridges… smell its piney scent… hum its anthem… shine the lamp") sustained across stanzas without break, while Berry deploys a three-phase narrative arc — accent locked away, accent leaked, accent reclaimed — patterned through stanza-breaks that mark each shift.

• Comparative evidence: Robinson's structural backbone runs "trace its ridges in your pocket" through "polish it / and let it shine" without rupture, whereas Berry's backbone moves from "in a box beneath the bed" through "We heard it escape sometimes" to "I wanted to forge your voice / in my mouth" across the poem's five stanzas.

• Comparative close analysis: Robinson's parallel imperative chain makes the structure itself the argument — the inheritance is so practised it cannot be paused — while Berry's three-phase arc makes the stanza-breaks the argument, the gaps between stanzas standing for the years during which the dialect was institutionally clamped.

• Comparative effects: Indeed the reader of Robinson experiences inheritance as a single sustained current the speaker is now passing forward, while the reader of Berry experiences inheritance as a recovered satisfaction earned across the poem's structural phases — current-of-practice contrasted with arc-of-recovery.

• Comparative author's purpose: Robinson can be read as structuring the poem to argue that interior practice is one of the resources British state failure cannot reach, while Berry may have been structuring the poem to argue that recovered voice carries the historical mark of the institution it had to survive.

• Comparative context: Robinson writes inside the post-Grenfell 2019 recognition that the British state had visibly failed Black Londoners — the seventy-two dead in the 2017 council-housing fire, the 2017-18 Windrush scandal — while Berry writes inside the post-1944 educational settlement in which Received Pronunciation was systematically promoted in state schools as the prestige variety and regional dialects (Black Country, Geordie, Scouse) were trained out of working-class children.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Robinson centres his closing argument on an amulet simile that pictures belonging as a small protective inherited object the speaker carries internally, Berry centres her closing argument on a homing-pigeon image that pictures belonging as an instinctual flight the dialect makes back to the body.

• Comparative technique: Robinson layers imperative verbs ("polish", "shine", "hum", "trace") with the closing simile of the amulet, while Berry layers compound nouns ("lock-jaw", "homing pigeons") with the homing-pigeon image and dialect lexis ("bostin' fittle", "cag-mag") absorbed unitalicised.

• Comparative evidence: Robinson closes "polish it / and let it shine like a brilliant amulet", while Berry closes "send your words, like pigeons, / fluttering for home" and earlier embeds "vowels ferrous as nails, consonants / you could lick the coal from".

• Comparative close analysis: Robinson's imperative "polish" and simile "like a brilliant amulet" together locate inheritance inside a centuries-old diasporic tradition of small protective inherited objects carried against hostile public cultures, while Berry's pigeon image and metallurgical (metal-working) lexis ("ferrous", "coal", "blacksmith's furnace") locate inheritance inside Black Country industrial labour and working-class pigeon-fancying.

• Comparative effects: Therefore Robinson's reader receives the inheritance as a private practice to be polished day after day, while Berry's reader receives it as a public instinct returning through the body — concealed amulet contrasted with fluttering bird.

• Comparative author's purpose: Robinson may have chosen the amulet language to argue that diasporic dignity is practised privately because the public sphere cannot be trusted, while Berry can be read as choosing the homing-pigeon language to argue that working-class regional voice belongs in public English literary writing rather than as italicised curiosity.

• Comparative context: Robinson writes inside the long Caribbean tradition of the jewel-and-amulet diasporic figure — the inheritance the migrant carries on the body across the Atlantic — while Berry writes inside the Black Country tradition in which pigeon-fancying remains a working-class pastime and the dialect descends from Old English and West Mercian with significant pre-Norman retention.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Robinson and Berry both locate belonging in a grandmother's inheritance carried into the speaker's present, but the institutional histories along which their inheritances stand produce opposite formal arcs: Robinson's continuous single-stanza instruction never breaks because the diaspora preserved the grandmother's practice, while Berry's broken-and-recovered bilingual free verse names the schoolroom's interruption and the dialect's eventual return.

• Controlling concept: Belonging in the anthology is not a fixed essence inherited from a single point of origin but, as Stuart Hall argues, "a positioning" — and both poems present that positioning through the figure of the grandmother as the main carrier of cultural inheritance whose voice the public sphere had refused.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Robinson can be read as writing inside the post-Grenfell recognition that interior practice is one of the resources British state failure cannot reach, while Berry may be writing inside the post-1944 recognition that the British literary tradition's idea of "correct" English has been a class-and-region project still in slow process of revision — both poets together refuse the picture of belonging as passport or birth certificate.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two poems reveals that the anthology's deepest move on belonging is the recognition that the place a person belongs to is something a previous generation handed forward and a present generation polishes — and that the reader, like the speaker, is the next person responsible for the practice.

Q2 — Thirteen (predicted named anchor) [HIGH confidence — core poem · race / Stephen Lawrence aftermath]

Extract

Anchor poem (printed on the AQA paper):

You will be four minutes from home

when you are cornered by an officer

who will tell you of a robbery, forty

minutes ago in the area. You fit

the description of a man? – You'll laugh.

Thirteen, you'll tell him: you're thirteen.

You'll be patted on the shoulder, then, by another fed

whose face takes you back to Gloucester Primary School,

a Wednesday assembly about being little stars.

This same officer had an horizon in the east

of his smile when he told your class that

Question

Compare how poets present ideas about race and identity in 'Thirteen' and in one other poem from 'Worlds and lives'. [30 marks]

Frame

Femi's 2020 reportorial lyric and Berry's 1990 narrative quatrain-poem both examine the racialised Black-British public encounter, but they sit at opposite ends of the anthology's half-century reach: Berry's 1955 train carries a Quaker woman's goodwill, while Femi's 2020 street carries the police stop. The anthology's editorial argument is the relationship between these moments.

The anthology's half-century reach (1955 to 2020) maps both continuity (the Black-British body remains the object of racialised public attention) and change (the goodwill of 1955 has not produced the structural change that would have removed the surveillance of 2020). Hall's "positioning" and Gilroy's "fundamentally Atlantic" frames travel cleanly across both poems.

• In medias res (Femi) — opening mid-action: "He was thirteen / walking home from school".

• Reportorial register (Femi) — plain declaratives refusing lyric ornament.

• Direct quotation (Berry) — the Quaker lady's question dropped unmediated.

• Conversational quatrains (Berry) — irregular tetrameter / pentameter, loose ABCB rhyme.

• Closing simile (Berry) — "it touched me like an act of love".

It frames the contemporary backdrop of Femi's poem two decades later, when Home Office figures still recorded Black Britons being stopped and searched at roughly eight to nine times the rate of their white counterparts.

Both poets present the Black-British encounter with the white British public sphere across the same axis but at different historical moments and with different state machinery active in the encounter.

• Thesis: Across form (reportorial free verse versus conversational quatrains), structure (in medias res — opening mid-action — progression to integrated normality versus a single moment recorded in five small movements), and language (the plain declarative refusing lyric ornament versus the closing simile honouring imperfect goodwill), Femi presents racialised contact as state surveillance built into ordinary Black boyhood, while Berry presents racialised contact as liberal goodwill real-and-partial enough to be received on its own terms. The comparison reveals the half-century reach of the anthology's argument about which racialised encounters British public space still produces.

• Comparative technique: Femi works inside the contemporary documentary-lyric tradition — plain declaratives, second-person address, no rhyme — whereas Berry works inside the post-Windrush conversational-lyric tradition — irregular tetrameter and pentameter, loose ABCB rhyme, direct quotation embedded in the speaker's recollection.

• Comparative technique: Femi uses a five-movement progression — walk, stop, identification, questioning, continued walking — patterned through the second-person turn, whereas Berry uses a single-scene expansion held in five quatrains — opening greeting, declaration, geographic confusion, snow-falls-elsewhere reply, closing valuation.

I heard a thousand blended notes,

While in a grove I sate reclined,

In that sweet mood when pleasant thoughts

Bring sad thoughts to the mind.

To her fair works did Nature link

The human soul that through me ran;

And much it grieved my heart to think

What man has made of man.

Through primrose tufts, in that green bower,

The periwinkle trailed its wreaths;

And 'tis my faith that every flower

Enjoys the air it breathes.

It seemed a thrill of pleasure.

The budding twigs spread out their fan,

To catch the breezy air;

And I must think, do all I can,

That there was pleasure there.

If such be Nature's holy plan,

Have I not reason to lament

What man has made of man?

Question

Compare how poets present ideas about place and nature in 'Lines Written in Early Spring' and in one other poem from 'Worlds and lives'. [30 marks]

Frame

Wordsworth's 1798 ballad-metre quatrains and Eliot's 1869 blank-verse anatomy both use a single national-political setting as the medium through which a structural argument about human living is delivered, but their conclusions diverge along the anthology's central country-and-city axis: Wordsworth's primrose grove invites moral questioning through what nature reveals about humanity, while Eliot's London drawing-room registers urban refusal through what the city blocks the speaker from seeing. The plan traces the difference between what the country includes and what the city refuses.

Pedagogical depth to thread. Both poems were written in moments of political pressure — Wordsworth at the end of the 1790s under the Combination Acts and post-revolutionary repression, Eliot in 1869 after the 1867 Second Reform Act and inside Britain's imperial-metropolitan moment. Both poets turn a single setting into a structural criticism of the whole British society around them. The anthology's editorial argument that nature poetry is never only nature poetry runs through both texts.

Technique gloss:

• Iambic tetrameter quatrains (Wordsworth) — folk-ballad metre married to philosophical content.

• Rhetorical question (Wordsworth) — "Have I not reason to lament / What man has made of man?" converts the poem from nature-lyric to political indictment.

• Pathetic fallacy (Wordsworth) — "every flower / Enjoys the air it breathes".

• Blank verse (Eliot) — unrhymed iambic pentameter, the form Milton and Wordsworth used for epic — deployed here for a confined interior.

• Negation cluster (Eliot) — "no", "nor", "not", "lowest rate" repeated as the poem's defining mode.

• Extended prison metaphor (Eliot) — "one huge prison-house and court".

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): In 1798, Wordsworth and Coleridge published the first edition of Lyrical Ballads — the volume usually dated as the start of English Romanticism. Within two years, the Combination Acts of 1799–1800 outlawed working-class political organisation, suspended habeas corpus, and criminalised the assembly of rural and urban poor that Wordsworth's primrose-grove ethical question quietly indicts. Seventy-one years later, George Eliot composed her blank-verse drawing-room anatomy in the imperial-metropolitan London of post-Reform 1869, where the Second Reform Act's 1867 expansion of male suffrage had only partially answered the question Wordsworth's 1798 poem had refused to let close.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Both poems were written by speakers who could have written reassuringly about their settings — Wordsworth in the Lake District's pantheist abundance, Eliot in the Regent's Park comfort of her partner's London home — and both poets refuse the reassurance, turning the small specific scene into structural indictment of the whole national arrangement that their settings sit inside.

• Thesis: Across form (folk-ballad quatrains versus blank verse), structure (sensory pleasure leading to moral questioning versus continuous accumulating negation refusing relief), and language (pantheist personification versus industrial-grey negation), Wordsworth presents place as the natural world's quiet indictment of "what man has made of man" while Eliot presents place as the urban world's structural refusal of colour, warmth, and joy. Both poets together argue that any honest attention to a national setting produces moral discomfort the surrounding political order would prefer to deny.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Wordsworth chooses iambic tetrameter quatrains in alternating ABAB rhyme to deliver structural criticism through a democratic folk-ballad form, Eliot chooses unrhymed iambic pentameter blank verse to deliver structural criticism through a form Milton and Wordsworth had used for epic.

• Comparative technique: Wordsworth works inside the Lyrical Ballads programme — folk-ballad metre married to philosophical content — whereas Eliot works inside the Victorian blank-verse meditation — the epic-philosophical form repurposed for a confined drawing-room interior.

• Comparative evidence: Wordsworth holds the form across six quatrains opening "I heard a thousand blended notes, / While in a grove I sate reclined" and closing "Have I not reason to lament / What man has made of man?", while Eliot holds her form across twenty-eight unrhymed lines opening "The sky is cloudy, yellowed by the smoke" and closing "With lowest rate of colour, warmth and joy".

• Comparative close analysis: Wordsworth's choice of folk-ballad form makes the regularity itself the argument — the closing question lands with the inescapability of hymn or nursery rhyme — while Eliot's choice of blank-verse form makes the form-content mismatch the argument, the epic measure deployed for the drawing-room making the room's airlessness more visible.

• Comparative effects: Thus the reader of Wordsworth feels the ethical pivot as a binding philosophical claim delivered in ballad-cadence, while the reader of Eliot feels the absence of stanza-break as enclosure inside the room's structural negation — democratic ethical pivot contrasted with epic-form refusal.

• Comparative author's purpose: Wordsworth may have chosen the folk-ballad form to argue that radical political content reaches further inside the most democratic of English forms, while Eliot can be read as choosing the blank-verse form to argue that the Victorian compromise her speaker inhabits cannot be redeemed by even the most elevated literary tradition.

• Comparative context: Wordsworth writes inside the 1798 inauguration of English Romanticism (Lyrical Ballads published the same year with Coleridge), at a moment when the Combination Acts of 1799-1800 would outlaw working-class political organisation and the suspension of habeas corpus criminalise political dissent. Eliot writes in 1869 at The Priory in Regent's Park, three years after the 1867 Second Reform Act had partially expanded male suffrage but left the structural inequalities her drawing-room records untouched.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Wordsworth structures the poem as four quatrains of sensory pleasure turning in the final two quatrains into rhetorical-question indictment, Eliot structures the poem as twenty-eight lines of continuous accumulating negation that refuses any volta (turn) or relief.

• Comparative technique: Wordsworth uses a pleasure-to-question pivot — four observational quatrains of primrose, periwinkle, birds, and budding twigs collapsing into the closing rhetorical question — whereas Eliot uses continuous accumulating negation across an unbroken paragraph — "no", "nor", "not", "lowest rate" recurring without sectional break.

• Comparative evidence: Wordsworth structurally pivots from "Through primrose tufts, in that green bower, / The periwinkle trailed its wreaths" into "Have I not reason to lament / What man has made of man?", while Eliot accumulates from "No bird can make a shadow as it flies, / For all is shadow" into "The world seems one huge prison-house and court".

• Comparative close analysis: Wordsworth's pivot from pastoral observation to rhetorical question makes the structural pleasure-to-questioning movement the argument — pleasure honestly attended produces the indictment — while Eliot's continuous accumulating negation makes the absence of pivot the argument, the room's airlessness so total no formal turn can produce relief.

• Comparative effects: Indeed the reader of Wordsworth is invited through sensory pleasure into moral discomfort, while the reader of Eliot is enclosed inside the room's accumulating negation without release — earned-pivot contrasted with refused-pivot.

• Comparative author's purpose: Wordsworth may have been structuring the poem to argue that the natural world's quiet abundance must be honestly attended before the political indictment can land, while Eliot can be read as structuring the poem to argue that the urban order's structural refusal of colour, warmth, and joy cannot be redeemed by any conventional formal turn.

• Comparative context: Wordsworth writes inside the Romantic pantheist inheritance that treats the natural world as morally continuous with the human, while Eliot writes inside the post-Reform Victorian recognition that imperial-metropolitan commerce had produced an interior airlessness the franchise extension of 1867 had not answered. • Comparative effects: Therefore Wordsworth's reader receives the natural world as moral teacher with which the human soul remains continuous, while Eliot's reader receives the urban world as commercial book-keeping where colour, warmth, and joy are reduced to "lowest rate" — moral-continuity contrasted with commercial-flattening.

• Comparative author's purpose: Wordsworth may have chosen the pantheist language to argue that natural abundance indicts the political order that has refused it to most humans, while Eliot can be read as choosing the industrial-grey language to name the moral cost of the Victorian compromise from inside the comfort that compromise produced.

• Comparative context: Wordsworth writes inside the late-1790s Romantic-pantheist inheritance shared with Coleridge's The Eolian Harp and the wider Lyrical Ballads project responding to French Revolution failure and Pittite repression, while Eliot writes inside the Victorian commercial-imperial vocabulary where book-keeping wording ("rate") had begun to take over the language of interior life under the pressure of industrial London.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Wordsworth and Eliot both turn a single national setting into structural criticism, but the country-and-city axis along which their settings stand produces opposite formal arcs: Wordsworth's ballad-metre quatrains earn moral questioning through pleasure, while Eliot's blank-verse paragraph refuses the relief any conventional turn would supply.

• Controlling concept: Place in the anthology is not a neutral background but the medium through which a structural argument about human living is delivered — and the rural-lyric and urban-lyric forms together stake the anthology's editorial claim that any honest attention to a national setting produces moral discomfort.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Wordsworth may have been arguing that pleasure honestly attended to is itself a political indictment, while Eliot can be read as examining the moral cost of the Victorian compromise from inside the comfort that compromise produced — both poets refuse the consolation their respective settings might have offered.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two poems reveals that the anthology's deepest move on place is the recognition that the country and the city are different vehicles for the same indictment — the British political and urban orders have refused different parts of the same flourishing — and the lyric form is the wording in which the refusal is most precisely named.

Question

Compare how poets present ideas about state power and its effects on the body in 'England in 1819' and in one other poem from 'Worlds and lives'. [30 marks]

Frame

Shelley's 1819 unpublishable sonnet and Femi's 2020 reportorial lyric both turn state violence into a structural argument across two centuries of British public life. The plan traces the long-historical-arc difference between the Peterloo Massacre of 16 August 1819 and the contemporary stop-and-search disparities the Macpherson Report named in 1999. The anthology's editorial argument that the indictment Shelley framed in 1819 has not yet been answered runs through both poems.

Pedagogical depth to thread. Both poets indict state violence on the body of the marginalised through forms the literary tradition had trained the reader to expect for elevated content — Shelley the sonnet of love, Femi the lyric of personal reflection. Both turn the formal expectation against the political content the poems actually deliver. The anthology's argument that the lyric form, properly directed, can outlast the political conditions it indicts runs across the two-century gap.

Technique gloss:

• Asyndetic catalogue (Shelley) — "old, mad, blind, despised, and dying" piles failing adjectives without coordinated relief.

• Extended parasitic simile (Shelley) — "leech-like to their fainting country cling".

• Conditional closing couplet (Shelley) — "a glorious Phantom may / Burst, to illumine our tempestuous day".

• In medias res (Femi) — "He was thirteen / walking home from school".

• Second-person address (Femi) — "you become a master of disguise".

• Plain declarative (Femi) — "he kept walking".

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): On 16 August 1819, yeomanry cavalry charged a peaceful working-class radical meeting at St Peter's Field in Manchester, killing eleven and wounding hundreds. On the streets of the same nation two centuries later, the Home Office's own statistics record Black Britons being stopped and searched at roughly eight to nine times the rate of their white counterparts. The violence Shelley named in his unpublishable 1819 sonnet finds a contemporary continuation in Femi's portrait of one thirteen-year-old's afternoon.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Both poets respond to specific state events that mainstream public debate had been reluctant to name — Shelley's libel-conscious editor Leigh Hunt refused to publish the sonnet in 1819, and Femi writes against the continuing British reluctance to name disproportionate stop-and-search as systemic. Both poems treat the lyric form as the available place for testimony when prose-political writing has been silenced or pushed aside.

• Thesis: Across form (broken sonnet versus reportorial free verse), structure (twelve-line catalogue collapsing into a conditional revolutionary "may" versus five-movement narrative building state contact into ordinary boyhood), and language (Latinate decay-imagery versus plain declarative), Shelley presents state power as an accumulated Regency failure that revolution may yet dismantle, while Femi presents it as state contact built into the ordinary experience of Black-British boyhood. The comparison presents the anthology's editorial argument that state violence on the body of the marginalised is the central moral question the British public sphere has refused to confront across two centuries.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Shelley chooses a broken sonnet form whose irregular rhyme scheme (ABABABCDCDCCDD) refuses both Petrarchan and Shakespearean closure, Femi chooses a reportorial free-verse form that refuses lyric ornament so the structural reality of state contact reads as ordinary.

• Comparative technique: Shelley works inside the Romantic radical-sonnet tradition — the fourteen-line form repurposed for political indictment when prose-political writing had been silenced by libel law — whereas Femi works inside the contemporary documentary-lyric tradition — plain declaratives, second-person address, no rhyme.

• Comparative evidence: Shelley holds his form across fourteen lines opening "An old, mad, blind, despised, and dying King" and closing "a glorious Phantom may / Burst, to illumine our tempestuous day", while Femi holds his form across stanzas opening "He was thirteen / walking home from school" and closing "he kept walking".

• Comparative close analysis: Shelley's choice of broken-sonnet form makes the rhyme-scheme irregularity itself the argument — the form formally homeless like the country it indicts — while Femi's choice of reportorial form makes the absence of rhyme and lyric flourish the argument, the encounter too structural for ornament.

• Comparative effects: Thus the reader of Shelley experiences twelve lines of suffocating catalogue followed by two lines of conditional hope inside the sonnet's architecture, while the reader of Femi receives the encounter as the new structural normal of Black boyhood — formal-suffocation contrasted with structural-normalisation.

• Comparative author's purpose: Shelley may have chosen the broken-sonnet form to turn the sonnet against the British state — the rulers who deserve a sonnet of praise have earned a sonnet of accusation — while Femi can be read as choosing the reportorial form to argue that institutional racism reads at the level of one ordinary afternoon.

• Comparative context: Shelley wrote in Italy in late 1819, weeks after receiving news of the Peterloo Massacre (16 August 1819, when yeomanry cavalry charged a peaceful working-class radical meeting at St Peter's Field in Manchester, killing eleven and wounding hundreds). Leigh Hunt refused to publish the poem in 1819 and it first appeared in 1839, seventeen years after Shelley's death. Femi writes inside the post-Macpherson recognition (the 1999 report named the Metropolitan Police "institutionally racist" after the Stephen Lawrence murder of 1993) that surveillance is structural.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Shelley structures the poem as a twelve-line asyndetic catalogue (a list without "and") of Regency failure that collapses into a conditional revolutionary couplet, Femi structures the poem as a five-movement narrative beginning in medias res and ending in integrated normality.

• Comparative technique: Shelley uses an accumulating catalogue — king, princes, rulers, people, army, laws, religion, senate — patterned across twelve lines before the closing conditional "may", whereas Femi uses a five-movement progression — walk, stop, identification, questioning, continued walking — patterned through the second-person turn.

• Comparative evidence: Shelley structurally pivots from "An old, mad, blind, despised, and dying King" through "A people starved and stabbed in th' untilled field" into "a glorious Phantom may / Burst", while Femi pivots from "He was thirteen / walking home from school" through "At thirteen you become a master of disguise" into "he kept walking".

• Comparative close analysis: Shelley's catalogue-into-conditional structure makes the architecture itself a political experience — the catalogue exhausts the reader before the conditional Phantom arrives, exactly mirroring the political condition described — while Femi's five-movement integration makes the structure the argument, the encounter producing not climax or resolution but the rerouted afternoon.

• Comparative effects: Indeed the reader of Shelley experiences accumulating suffocation followed by conditional hope, while the reader of Femi experiences mundane recognition followed by integrated normality — prophetic-couplet contrasted with integrated-normal.

• Comparative author's purpose: Shelley may have been structuring the poem to argue that Regency Britain's failures cumulatively justify a conditional revolutionary hope, while Femi can be read as structuring the poem to argue that contemporary state contact builds into ordinary boyhood without dramatic resolution.

• Comparative context: Shelley writes inside the post-Peterloo Romantic radical criticism of monarchy and the libel laws that prevented publication — Queen Mab (1813) and Mask of Anarchy (1819) sit alongside this sonnet as Shelley's radical project. • Comparative evidence: Shelley's lexical signature runs "old, mad, blind, despised, and dying" through "leech-like to their fainting country cling" to "a glorious Phantom may / Burst", while Femi's runs "He was thirteen" through "you become a master of disguise" to "he kept walking".

• Comparative effects: Therefore Shelley's reader receives state violence through Latinate exhaustion followed by conditional hope, while Femi's reader receives state violence through forced inhabitation of the boy's afternoon — Romantic-prophetic style contrasted with documentary-coercion style.

• Comparative author's purpose: Shelley may have chosen the Latinate language to mark that the violence demanded invented vocabulary (literary tradition had no adequate word for the yeomanry's charge), while Femi can be read as choosing the plain declarative to mark that the violence is precisely too ordinary for invention.

• Comparative context: Shelley writes inside the Romantic radical-prophetic style shared with Byron's Don Juan and Blake's Songs of Experience — the lyric turned against state machinery when prose-political writing has been silenced. Femi writes inside the contemporary documentary-lyric inheritance shaped by Claudia Rankine's Citizen and the broader Black-British poetic refusal of dramatic resolution.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Shelley and Femi both indict state violence on the body of the marginalised, but the two centuries separating them produce opposite formal arcs: Shelley's broken sonnet catalogues accumulated Regency failure into a conditional revolutionary "may", while Femi's reportorial free verse builds contemporary state contact into the single specific case of one thirteen-year-old's afternoon.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Shelley may have been arguing that Regency Britain's political failures cumulatively justify a conditional revolutionary hope, while Femi can be read as arguing that contemporary Britain's policing practices cumulatively form a structural violation — the two purposes coming together on the idea that the lyric is the form best placed to make state violence visible.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two poems reveals that the anthology's deepest move on power is the recognition that the indictments framed two centuries apart have not yet been answered — and that the reader, refusing the comfort of historical distance, is asked to take present accountability for the violence the British public sphere has continued to refuse to name openly.

• Comparative evidence: Mundair's lexical signature runs "Like Rama I have felt the Wilderness" through "the Punjabi in my mouth / became dislodged" to "void of history and memory", while Berry's runs "the lock-jaw of the schoolroom" through "vowels ferrous as nails, consonants / you could lick the coal from" to "my tongue / a flock of homing pigeons".

• Comparative close analysis: Mundair's botanical phrase "infertile English soil" names the institutional refusal to nourish the inherited tongue while the verb "dislodged" gives the violation a quiet violence (the milk-teeth analogy infusing the moment with childhood vulnerability). Berry's compound noun "lock-jaw" names the schoolroom as mouth-clamping device and the homing-pigeon image turns the dialect from suppressed voice into instinctual return.

• Comparative effects: Therefore Mundair's reader receives identity through accumulating mythic and botanical loss-imagery, while Berry's reader receives identity through industrial and avian recovery-imagery — mythic-loss contrasted with industrial-return.

• Comparative author's purpose: Mundair may have chosen the Hindu-mythic and botanical language to argue that the bilingual interior carries inheritances the dominant English vocabulary refuses to acknowledge, while Berry can be read as choosing the lock-jaw and homing-pigeon language to argue that working-class regional voice belongs in public English literary writing rather than as italicised curiosity.

• Comparative context: Mundair writes inside the contemporary South Asian-British inheritance shaped by Imtiaz Dharker, Daljit Nagra, and the wider postcolonial code-switching tradition. Berry writes inside the Black Country tradition in which pigeon-fancying remains a working-class pastime and the dialect descends from Old English and West Mercian with significant pre-Norman retention.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Mundair and Berry both present identity as language carried in the body and reclaimed against institutional suppression, but the formal moments along which their reclamations stand produce parallel arcs across opposite styles: Mundair's triptych examines violation-to-reclamation across three movements, while Berry's free verse presents loss-and-return across stanzas.

• Controlling concept: Identity in the anthology is, as Stuart Hall argues, "a positioning" rather than a fixed essence inherited from a single point of origin — and both poems stake the claim that the inherited bilingual or dialect tongue is one of the legitimate styles of English literary writing rather than deviation from a "correct" English.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Mundair can be read as arguing that the bilingual mouth is doubled rather than divided, while Berry may have been arguing that the British literary tradition has been too narrow about which voices count as English literature — both poets refuse the standardising assumption.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two poems reveals that the anthology's deepest move on identity is the recognition that the British public sphere's previous idea of "correct" English has been a class-and-region-and-race project — and that the reclamation of inherited tongues is the slow contemporary process by which the project is being revised.

Q10 — On an Afternoon Train from Purley to Victoria, 1955 (predicted named anchor) [MED confidence — non-spine · migration / dignity in the diaspora]

Extract

Anchor poem (printed on the AQA paper):

Hello, she said, and startled me.

Nice day. Nice day I agreed.

I am a Quaker she said and Sunday

I was moved in silence

to speak a poem loudly

for racial brotherhood.

I was thoughtful, then said

what poem came on like that?

One the moment inspired she said.

I was again thoughtful.

Inexplicably I saw

empty city streets lit dimly

in a day's first hours.

Alongside in darkness

was my father's big banana field.

Where are you from? she said.

Jamaica I said.

What part of Africa is Jamaica? she said.

Where Ireland is near Lapland I said.

Hard to see why you leave

such sunny country she said.

Snow falls elsewhere I said.

So sincere she was beautiful

as people sat down around us.

— James Berry, On an Afternoon Train from Purley to Victoria, 1955 (1995)

Suggested comparison poem (student choice — pick any other Worlds and Lives poem): A Portable Paradise by Roger Robinson. Why: pairs Caribbean-British dignity at opposite ends of the post-Windrush half-century — Berry's 1955 cross-racial public encounter against Robinson's 2019 interior practice — both poets refusing the binary between bitterness and gratitude.

Alternative comparison candidates:

• Thirteen (Femi) — pairs cross-racial recognition with state surveillance across seven decades; same Black-British public-sphere axis.

• Name Journeys (Mundair) — diaspora dignity carried in name and tongue; pairs Caribbean and South Asian migrant interiors.

• Homing (Berry) — Black Country dialect-as-inheritance pairs with Berry's own Caribbean-British train encounter (same surname — different poet, no relation).

Question

Compare how poets present ideas about migration and dignity in the diaspora in 'On an Afternoon Train from Purley to Victoria, 1955' and in one other poem from 'Worlds and lives'. [30 marks]

Frame

Berry's 1990 narrative quatrain-poem and Robinson's 2019 single-stanza meditation both present dignity in the Caribbean-British diaspora, but at opposite ends of the post-Windrush half-century: Berry's 1955 train carries cross-racial recognition, Robinson's 2019 pocket carries the grandmother's instruction. Both poets refuse the binary between bitterness and gratitude.

Pedagogical depth to thread. Both poets refuse to turn the migrant condition into either sentimentality or grievance. Berry's speaker accepts the Quaker lady's gesture for what it intended ("an act of love"); Robinson's speaker polishes the grandmother's instruction across the diaspora. The anthology's editorial argument that dignity in the diaspora is a present-tense practice — the moment-by-moment choice of how to receive or carry the new world — runs across both poems.

Technique gloss:

• Direct quotation (Berry) — the Quaker lady's question dropped unmediated.

• Conversational quatrains (Berry) — irregular tetrameter / pentameter, loose ABCB rhyme.

• Closing simile (Berry) — "it touched me like an act of love".

• Imperative mood (Robinson) — "take", "keep", "polish".

• Amulet simile (Robinson) — "like a brilliant amulet".

• Hook (historical fact tied to thesis): The Society of Friends — Quakers — had been the first major British religious denomination to oppose the transatlantic slave trade, beginning in the 1670s with the Germantown Friends' anti-slavery petition and continuing through the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. By 1955, when James Berry recorded his commuter-train encounter on the Purley-to-Victoria line, Quakers were among the relatively few white Britons engaged in active anti-racist work. The lady's gift of a worn-leather Bible carries that two-century history into the post-Windrush moment.

• Building sentence (anthology context + comparison axis named): Roger Robinson, writing thirty years later in 2019 from the Trinidadian-British diaspora in which the 2017–18 Windrush scandal had wrongly classified longstanding Black Britons as illegal immigrants, uses his Trinidadian grandmother's portable-paradise instruction to name an adjacent dignity — the meditative practice carried in an inside jacket pocket. Both poets present dignity in the diaspora at opposite ends of the post-Windrush half-century.

• Thesis: Across form (conversational quatrains versus single-stanza unbroken instruction), structure (a single moment recorded in five small movements versus continuous grandmother-instruction sustaining the practice), and language (the closing simile of imperfect goodwill versus the closing simile of the amulet inheritance), Berry presents dignity in the diaspora as the small public choice to receive imperfect goodwill on its own terms, while Robinson presents it as the continuous interior practice the grandmother transmitted across the diaspora. The comparison reveals the anthology's editorial argument that dignity in the Caribbean-British diaspora is a present-tense practice across the half-century reach.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Berry chooses a conversational quatrain form whose irregular tetrameter / pentameter and loose ABCB rhyme allow the speaker to record a public encounter without polemic or sentiment, Robinson chooses a single-stanza meditative free-verse form that holds the grandmotherly counsel unbroken across the diaspora.

• Comparative technique: Berry works inside the post-Windrush conversational-lyric tradition — looser quatrains, direct quotation embedded in the speaker's recollection — whereas Robinson works inside the meditative counsel-poem tradition — one continuous stanza of imperative instruction without sectional break.

• Comparative evidence: Berry opens "Hello, she said, and startled me" and closes "So sincere she was beautiful / as people sat down around us" across his looser quatrains, while Robinson opens "And if I speak of Paradise, / then I'm speaking of my grandmother" and closes "polish it / and let it shine like a brilliant amulet" inside one unbroken stanza.

• Comparative close analysis: Berry's choice of conversational-quatrain form makes the looser rhyme itself the argument — the half-formal cadence honours the lady's gesture without turning it into hymn or polemic — while Robinson's choice of meditative-counsel form makes the absence of stanza breaks the argument, the grandmother's voice never interrupted because the diaspora preserved it.

• Comparative effects: Thus the reader of Berry receives dignity as the small public choice to engage rather than retreat, while the reader of Robinson receives dignity as continuous interior practice passed across one breath — public-engagement contrasted with interior-practice.

• Comparative author's purpose: Berry may have chosen the conversational form to argue that liberal goodwill in 1955 deserved to be recorded for what it intended rather than dismissed for what it could not yet deliver, while Robinson can be read as choosing the meditative-counsel form to argue that interior practice is one of the resources British state failure cannot reach.

• Comparative context: Berry writes inside the post-Windrush 1955 moment — three years before the Notting Hill riots of 1958, seven years before the first Commonwealth Immigrants Act of 1962 — when the Quaker tradition (first major British denomination to oppose the slave trade, beginning in the 1670s with the Germantown petition) was among the few white-British public currents engaged in active anti-racist work. Robinson writes inside the post-Grenfell Caribbean-British diaspora in which the 2017 fire and the 2017-18 Windrush scandal had reopened the British state's relationship to its Black citizens.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Berry structures the poem as a single moment expanded across five quatrain-movements of greeting, declaration, geographic confusion, snow-falls-elsewhere reply, and closing valuation, Robinson structures the poem as one continuous imperative transmission moving from grandmother to speaker to reader without break.

• Comparative technique: Berry uses a single-scene expansion held in five quatrains — opening greeting, the lady's Quaker self-introduction, geographic question, the speaker's punchline reply, closing valuation — whereas Robinson uses cumulative imperative parallelism ("trace… smell… hum… polish") sustained across stanzas without break.

• Comparative evidence: Berry pivots from "Hello, she said, and startled me" through "What part of Africa is Jamaica? she said. / Where Ireland is near Lapland I said" into "So sincere she was beautiful", while Robinson runs from "trace its ridges in your pocket" through "smell its piney scent" to "polish it / and let it shine".

• Comparative close analysis: Berry's single-encounter expansion makes the small physical movements the argument — the lady's gestures and the speaker's chosen reply weighed as the poem's emotional architecture — while Robinson's parallel imperative chain makes the absence of breaks the argument, the inheritance practised so continuously it cannot be paused.

• Comparative effects: Indeed the reader of Berry receives dignity as the speaker's chosen reception of imperfect goodwill, while the reader of Robinson experiences dignity as a single sustained current the speaker is now passing forward — chosen-reception contrasted with current-of-practice.

• Comparative author's purpose: Berry can be read as structuring the poem to argue that dignity in the Caribbean-British diaspora is a moment-by-moment choice the speaker makes inside small public encounters, while Robinson may have been structuring the poem to argue that diasporic dignity must be transmitted as continuous practice rather than interrupted gift.

• Comparative context: Berry writes from the 1948-arrived Windrush generation's first decade in Britain, when the demographic transformation produced by Empire's collapse was first showing up in the British public sphere — the Purley-to-Victoria line itself ran from suburban south London into central London at the moment when commuter encounters with Caribbean migrants were becoming routine. Robinson writes inside the long Caribbean tradition of grandmother-wisdom poetry treating the elderly woman as the main carrier of cultural inheritance across the Middle Passage and Windrush voyage.

• Comparative topic sentence: While Berry centres his closing argument on a simile of imperfect goodwill alongside direct quotation that honours the lady's gesture without flattening its political limits, Robinson centres his closing argument on an imperative verb and amulet simile that locates dignity inside a centuries-old diasporic tradition.

• Comparative technique: Berry layers unmediated direct quotation with closing simile and geographic punchline — "'Where do you come from?'" alongside "Where Ireland is near Lapland" and "like an act of love" — whereas Robinson layers cumulative imperative verbs with the closing amulet simile — "polish" alongside "like a brilliant amulet".

• Comparative evidence: Berry's lexical signature runs "'Hello'" through "'Where do you come from?'" to "So sincere she was beautiful / as people sat down around us", while Robinson's runs "trace its ridges in your pocket" through "smell its piney scent" to "polish it / and let it shine like a brilliant amulet".

• Comparative close analysis: Berry's adjective "sincere" honours the lady's gesture for what it intended while the closing line "as people sat down around us" names the public weight of the small encounter. Robinson's verb "polish" and simile "like a brilliant amulet" together locate dignity inside the centuries-old diasporic figure of the small protective inherited object.

• Comparative effects: Therefore Berry's reader receives dignity through modelled reception of imperfect goodwill, while Robinson's reader receives dignity as private practice to be polished day after day — modelled-reception contrasted with private-practice.

• Comparative author's purpose: Berry may have chosen the direct quotation and closing simile to argue that imperfect cross-racial goodwill is worth accepting on its own terms while structural change continues to be the longer work, while Robinson can be read as choosing the imperative and amulet language to argue that interior practice is one of the resources British state failure cannot reach.

• Comparative context: Berry writes inside the Caribbean conversational-lyric inheritance shaped by Louise Bennett, Edward Kamau Brathwaite, and the wider Atlantic conversational-lyric tradition Paul Gilroy names "constitutively Atlantic and transnational". Robinson writes inside the long Caribbean tradition of the jewel-and-amulet diasporic figure carried on the body across the Atlantic.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Berry and Robinson both present dignity in the Caribbean-British diaspora, but the half-century separating them produces opposite formal arcs: Berry's conversational quatrains record dignity in the small public choice to receive imperfect goodwill, while Robinson's single-stanza instruction transmits dignity as continuous interior practice across the diaspora.

• Controlling concept: Dignity in the anthology is, as Paul Gilroy argues, fundamentally Atlantic and transnational — not a state to be granted but a present-tense practice the diasporic speaker chooses, whether on a 1955 commuter train or in a 2019 inside jacket pocket.

• Authors' purposes contrasted: Berry may have been honouring the partial goodwill of the Windrush generation's first decades, while Robinson can be read as arguing that interior practice is one of the resources British state failure cannot reach — both poets refuse the binary that contemporary debate on race typically offers.

• Ultimate moral about what comparing them reveals: Comparing the two poems reveals that the anthology's deepest move on migration is the recognition that dignity is a moment-by-moment choice — and that the anthology's half-century reach maps both continuity (the Caribbean-British speaker's dignity sustained against the British public sphere) and change (the public choice of 1955 has become the interior practice of 2019 because the post-Grenfell state can no longer be trusted as the partner of recognition).

Two critical voices to deploy

Sophicly deploys two named critical voices across the W&L corpus. Use them sparingly — once per essay maximum — and only when they sharpen the close analysis.

• Stuart Hall on diaspora and identity. Attribute as "Jamaican-British cultural theorist Stuart Hall" or simply "Hall". The reading: identity is "always a positioning" rather than a fixed essence inherited from a single point of origin (per his 1990 essay Cultural Identity and Diaspora); diasporic identity is "the experience of staying and the experience of leaving" carried simultaneously. Use in any Q where contemporary identity politics is the analytical event — particularly Q1, Q2, Q6, Q9, Q10.

• Paul Gilroy on the Black Atlantic and modernity. Attribute as "British cultural theorist Paul Gilroy" or simply "Gilroy". The reading: Black-British and Black-American culture are "constitutively Atlantic and transnational" rather than minor footnotes to European modernity (per his 1993 book The Black Atlantic: Modernity and Double Consciousness); the Atlantic crossing is the central modern experience. Use in any Q where the anthology's contemporary Black-British poetry is the focus — particularly Q1, Q2, Q7, Q10.

(Attribute readings to these named voices; do not name Mr Bruff in essays. Bruff is a teaching guide for revision, not an academic source for citation.)

Final reminder bullet

• Name WHICH world, name WHICH life. Worlds and Lives is plural on both sides; every comparison thesis must specify the world the speaker inhabits and the life that world produces. Generic answers cap at Band 4.

• Hall + Gilroy on diaspora positioning. Use sparingly in any contemporary-half comparison; once per essay maximum.

• Robinson's "internal archive" frame. Heritage is portable, inherited, and practised — not a destination but a discipline.

• One anchor quote per BP — single line, embodying the topic-sentence concept.

• Discourse markers when switching poets — Thus / Whereas / By contrast / However / Indeed / Similarly / In conclusion.

• Hedged author's-purpose claims — may have / can be read as / a defensible reading is.

• No "shows" as analytical verb — illustrates / portrays / registers / enacts / dramatises / exposes / reveals / conveys / mirrors / signals.

• No arrows — through / from / leading to / which produces.

• No feminist-critique framework — reframe gender as social order / honour culture / paternal authority / institutional practice.

• No explicit sexual references — audience is 13-16; reframe Victorian-era references in legal-status / social-vulnerability terms.

• No magic / spellbook framing — drop spellbook / magic / spell / incantation across all copy.

• No "patriarchy" / "patriarchal" — use society / social order / paternal / Elizabethan-or-Victorian substitutes.

• Academic register throughout — no contractions, no colloquialisms, no chatty asides, no exclamation marks outside quoted text.

• Hook must tie thesis — fact / quote / question / metaphor that encapsulates THIS essay's specific argument.

• Sentence cap ~35-45 words / ~3 lines. Split rather than truncate.

• Topic sentences are CONCEPT-FIRST — no techniques named in sentence 1; do not even name forms.
